title: Podcast with Helmut Katzgraber, General partner at 55North - Quantum Computing Report
gist: Helmut Katzgraber (55North GP, ex-AWS/Microsoft quantum lead) argues near-to-mid-term quantum value will come from quantum simulation of strongly correlated materials, chemicals and pharma rather than optimisation, that enabling technologies (refrigeration, error-correction software) offer the clearest VC-timeline returns, and that Europe has world-class talent but a leaky lab-to-product pipeline and a growth-capital gap versus the US.

themes.theme: Where quantum will (and won't) deliver value

insights.claim: The first real-world quantum impact is most likely in materials, chemicals and pharma requiring quantum-mechanical simulation, not in optimization, where classical methods are expected to outperform quantum devices for the foreseeable future.
insights.speaker: Helmut Katzgraber
insights.so_what: Weight quantum exposure toward simulation/chemistry plays and discount optimization-marketed quantum companies; the speaker is a known optimization skeptic with AWS/Microsoft pedigree.
insights.quote: in optimization, I’m relatively bullish that classical technologies for the foreseeable future will outperform any quantum device.

insights.claim: Strongly correlated systems such as battery materials, organic LEDs, and metal-organic-complex medications are where classical compute struggles most and where quantum simulation is most likely to add value.
insights.speaker: Helmut Katzgraber
insights.so_what: Specific high-value verticals to track for early quantum simulation milestones: batteries, OLEDs, metal-organic pharma.
insights.quote: when you’re simulating strongly correlated things, you know, like battery materials, organic LEDs, you know, certain medications that have metal organic complexes. That’s where classical compute struggles
insights.locator: in material science, this is where the hope is

insights.claim: Quantum machine learning is too nascent for the speaker to commit a view, signaling it remains an unproven application class relative to simulation.
insights.speaker: Helmut Katzgraber
insights.so_what: Treat quantum-ML pitches as exploratory; an experienced practitioner explicitly declines to back the category yet.
insights.quote: machine learning, I would say I don’t want to hedge my bets yet simply because, you know, it’s still relatively nascent

insights.claim: Enabling technologies that many modalities will need, such as refrigeration and error-correction software, can deliver returns within typical VC timelines, whereas applications are too early for industrial-scale impact.
insights.speaker: Helmut Katzgraber
insights.so_what: Cross-modality enabling-tech (cryogenics, error-correction software) is the lower-risk, sooner-return quantum bet versus full-stack hardware or applications.
insights.quote: Think of enabling technologies, refrigeration, for example, something that many modalities will need, or software to compute error correction models.
insights.locator: Do you think the returns for venture for quantum investments

insights.claim: Error-correction software is becoming a universal need as the field moves from physical to logical qubits, making it a broadly applicable investment regardless of which hardware modality wins.
insights.speaker: Helmut Katzgraber
insights.so_what: The physical-to-logical transition creates pull-through demand for error-correction software; a modality-agnostic picks-and-shovels play.
insights.quote: especially now that we move from physical to logical qubits, something that everybody’s gonna need.
insights.locator: Do you think the returns for venture

insights.claim: Non-big-tech full-stack quantum companies have a relatively small probability of success, and the market will eventually coalesce, with surviving full-stack startups growing while others pivot to focusing on components.
insights.speaker: Helmut Katzgraber
insights.so_what: Expect consolidation and component specialization in quantum hardware; few full-stack startups survive, so favor component leaders or eventual acquirers.
insights.quote: you can count on your both hands the companies that really have a good shot at succeeding versus all the others that are trying and eventually end up pivoting
insights.locator: Do you think full stack companies

insights.claim: 55North backed IQM, which the speaker cites as a recent example of a potential unicorn that might bring returns sooner than typical quantum timelines.
insights.speaker: Helmut Katzgraber
insights.so_what: IQM is flagged as a near-term return candidate in the European quantum hardware space.
insights.quote: there’s always the one or the other unicorn like IQM recently that had us back, that might bring returns sooner and later.
insights.locator: We have a long-term perspective with unpatient capital

insights.claim: Europe has top-tier quantum talent and a strong lab ecosystem but a leaky lab-to-product pipeline, and lags the US specifically on growth capital and venture/product mindset.
insights.speaker: Helmut Katzgraber
insights.so_what: European quantum upside is gated by capital and commercialization, not science; watch for EU policy (Proposition 28) and growth-capital formation as the unlock.
insights.quote: while we have the great ideas, what we’re lacking is the venture mindset in Europe. We’re lacking the product mindset, and more importantly, we’re lacking the growth capital
insights.locator: do you think Europe can compete with the US and with China

insights.claim: Export-control laws force VCs investing in US quantum companies to hold only a small fraction of equity to avoid triggering restrictions, and active-conflict-zone countries are off-limits, creating cross-border friction in quantum dealmaking.
insights.speaker: Helmut Katzgraber
insights.so_what: Quantum is increasingly treated as strategic/dual-use; cross-border quantum investments carry regulatory structuring costs and the risk of symmetric EU restrictions on US firms.
insights.quote: when we invest in the US, we have to be careful that we have just a small fraction of the company after the investment, so that we don’t trigger export control laws.
insights.locator: How are you thinking about the geopolitics of quantum

insights.claim: Quantum computers should be treated as a new compute modality accessible like FPGAs or GPUs in the cloud, but require a software stack built for hybrid quantum-classical workloads with tight integration.
insights.speaker: Helmut Katzgraber
insights.so_what: The hybrid quantum-classical software integration layer (e.g. Azure Quantum Elements combining AI compute and quantum) is where cloud providers add value and where investment opportunity sits.
insights.quote: I think Microsoft did something interesting with Azure Quantum Elements where they combined AI compute and quantum in an effort to basically make an end-to-end chemistry pipeline.
insights.locator: how should a classical cloud provider adjust

insights.claim: Most quantum machines will likely remain cloud-hosted for the foreseeable future because they require heavy babysitting and maintenance, though some institutions (e.g. national intelligence, HPC centers like Juelich, Denmark's QuNorth) will run on-prem for specific reasons.
insights.speaker: Helmut Katzgraber
insights.so_what: Cloud access remains the dominant quantum delivery model; on-prem demand is a niche tied to sovereignty/security, mirroring HPC's cloud-plus-on-prem split.
insights.quote: I suspect that a lot of them will remain in the cloud. And it has mostly to do with the fact that... It requires a lot more babysitting for the foreseeable future.
insights.locator: do you see them shifting more into on-premises

insights.claim: No quantum modality has a sizable advantage over others: superconducting suits fast variational workloads, neutral atoms suit high qubit counts, and ion traps suit very high quality qubits, so the right modality is the one that best fits a specific application.
insights.speaker: Helmut Katzgraber
insights.so_what: Avoid betting on a single winning modality; value accrues to application-fit, supporting a diversified or component-level investment thesis.
insights.quote: my favorite modality is the one that brings most value for the problem you’re trying to look at.

insights.claim: Custom solutions targeting a specific problem (e.g. simulating strongly correlated battery materials) will always outperform generic tools, but the speaker judges it too early to commit machines to narrow use cases while hardware is still in its infancy.
insights.speaker: Helmut Katzgraber
insights.so_what: Problem-first vertical quantum products are the long-run winners, but the timing is premature; expect a phase of generic experimentation before verticalization.
insights.quote: custom solutions to difficult problems will always outperform any kind of generic tool.

insights.claim: 55North is a Denmark-based quantum VC with a 134 million euro first close, capacity to grow to 300 million euros, investing pre-seed to Series B across computing, sensing and networking globally, backed by LPs including the Novo Nordisk Foundation and EIFO.
insights.speaker: Helmut Katzgraber
insights.so_what: A concrete map of European quantum capital: 55North's stage, size, scope, and institutional LPs signal where Nordic quantum dealflow is being funded.
insights.quote: the first close was 134 million euros, with the goal being to focus on all sorts of quantum technologies and adjacent technologies. The fund can grow up to 300 million euros.

insights.claim: The biggest workforce gap in quantum is a product mindset, and hybrid physics-plus-business/product-management degrees would be extremely valuable because startups have great ideas but cannot scale to manufacturing.
insights.speaker: Helmut Katzgraber
insights.so_what: A structural talent bottleneck (commercialization/product skills, not physics) is gating quantum company scaling; a signal for where the sector's execution risk lies.
insights.quote: What I would love to see more is a product mindset... those hybrid degrees, business and quantum, I think are the ones that we need.
insights.locator: Workforce development has been spoken about
